data.epi_content:
The horses pulling the carriage snorted and released steam, as if expressing their frustration from the tiring journey.

The place where the carriage arrived wasn't some eerie location like a cemetery or columbarium. It was a small building attached to a medical clinic supposedly run by the son of the Emperor's personal physician. It was a place for those who had died with slightly horrific appearances rather than in peaceful rest while receiving treatment.

The Crown Prince was waiting for them, sitting in a chair made of shadows with his legs crossed, burning tobacco in his pipe. When Vivian and Evan tried to bow respectfully, the Crown Prince waved his hand dismissively as if such formalities didn't matter.

"Just open this door and go in a little further, and you'll find your friend beautifully prepared. I've entrusted her to the Empire's most meticulous mortician."

"So, can we go in right now?"

"No, tell me what you intend to do first. If you're planning to resurrect the corpse, create a new life form using that woman's body, or do something that necromancers or Black Magic practitioners might do... well, I'd have to lay you down right next to your friend."

"I'm just going to touch her head a little."

"Touch? You mean her memories?"

Evan nodded.

Vivian somehow had suspected this might be the case, but hearing it directly filled her with both sadness and anger. How was rummaging through someone's mind to peek at their memories any different from grave robbing—digging up a tomb to examine what's inside?

But judging from their demeanor, it didn't seem like something Vivian could prevent even if she tried to intervene.

"It's already difficult to read the memories of a living person. Do you think you can read the memories of a dead one?"

The Crown Prince inhaled smoke from his pipe, then made a choking sound in his throat, as if some noxious herb had been mixed with the tobacco. After coughing and spitting out phlegm, he cleared his throat.

"I might be able to, but if it doesn't work, I'll have to give up."

"I suppose so. Don't worry. If you try to turn that corpse into some kind of undead servant, my friend who made this chair will instantly blow your head off. I'll make sure you don't succumb to temptation!"

The Crown Prince spoke as if it were a hilarious joke, as though he had seen countless fools trying to revive the dead.

"So, Vivian. Why have you come?"

"I'm going in with Evan."

"This is somewhat awkward to say, but it will be disgusting. Not just the smell, but watching someone poke around those pink brain bits will be quite an ordeal."

"I don't mind."

"If that's the case. I pray you won't do anything disrespectful to the deceased."

It was contradictory. Inviting someone who said they would fiddle with a dead person's head, allowing them to achieve their goal, while hoping they wouldn't do anything disrespectful. What did he consider disrespectful?
data.epi_content2:
Vivian pondered this thought. But she could only think about it, not oppose or block it. It was obvious that if she objected, she would be told to take the carriage back.

The Crown Prince inhaled smoke, remembering his sister who once moved as if alive, but had long been dead and emitting a putrid smell. He recalled how he had once burned her—whether she had been alive or dead, he wasn't sure what word to use.

He didn't inhale deeply. The smoke from the lightly processed leaves was far too harsh.

...

Erica lay there. Her red blood-soaked uniform was gone, replaced by pure white clothes on a cold metal bed. The hole in her temple, large enough to fit a finger, was covered with gauze and blocked with something soft.

Evan walked up to Erica, removed the gauze, and peeled away the soft material with his bare hands as if it were nothing.

Then, with the distinctive smell of blood, some of the contents slightly spurted out.

"Ugh... urp!"

"Vivian, if it's disgusting, you can wait outside."

"Evan, do you really think this is the right thing to do?"

"No."

Before Vivian could continue, he inserted his finger into the head and sent a slight electrical current through it.

Magicians, being such a diverse bunch of eccentrics, had pursued and accumulated vast knowledge. One Black Magic practitioner, curious about the human brain, discovered which parts of the brain stored memories, processed thoughts, and exercised imagination.

Although that person was burned to death, his knowledge was successfully preserved. It was too valuable to be dismissed as evil and burned along with its discoverer.

Evan used that knowledge to find the hippocampus—swimming in the sea of information that was the brain—poked it with his finger, and began to recite a spell. At a glance, it looked like a Black Magic practitioner performing some terrible ritual with a sacrifice, making Vivian increasingly confused as she watched.

The Crown Prince had clearly said he would blow Evan's head off if he did anything like a necromancer or Black Magic practitioner would do. If this was considered normal, what on earth did normalcy mean to them?

For Vivian, who had spent most of her life trying to see only the good and live positively, this was something she couldn't even begin to imagine.

When Evan finished reciting the spell, Erica's body trembled, and a screen appeared above her.

[Sigh... I hate this.]

The image showed Erica entering a bathtub filled with steaming hot water while fully clothed. She took a blade and stabbed her wrist. Instantly, the clear water in the tub began to turn red like spreading paint.

[...Argh... damn it...]
data.epi_content3:
She trembled with her hand, tried to slash her wrist again, then threw the blade away and picked up a pistol.

It was the same pistol she had used to kill Lydia and blow her own head off. When she pulled the trigger, instead of her head being blown off like before, the bullet merely grazed her throat and left a red mark on the wall.

After that, Erica died very slowly, with no one looking for her, in a very lonely and painful way. The vision showed a person dying gradually—crying, trying unsuccessfully to get out of the tub, considering shooting herself in the head again, fumbling on the floor with her still-functioning right hand for the gun, seeing it fallen to her left, and giving up in despair. With the sound of her mumbling something, the screen went dark, and the vision ended.

It was brief, yet it felt long. More accurately, the impact of the vision was far greater than its duration. Of course, if you asked Erica about her most intense memory after experiencing such a harsh death, it would inevitably be her first death.

That's why Vivian just cried watching it. Although complex and terrible emotions crept up her spine, it wasn't a situation where she could say anything. So she continued her emotional outburst—wailing, sobbing, crying, screaming—loud enough that the cold corpses around them might wake up.

Seeing this, Evan's legs gave way. It was an absurd thought, but the idea he had conjured up to escape reality was actually true.

Though he knew it was already too late, and it might be some strange phenomenon beyond his control, he wanted to do something, anything.

But he was not one of those great magicians who appear once every thousand years, capable of turning back time or sending messages to their past selves.

He created water out of thin air to wash his hands, then filled the hole in Erica's head.

Then he helped the crying Vivian up and led her out of the building.

Erica's brain no longer held any value. The remaining fragments contained nothing but the most intense memories—it was essentially just a pile of waste.

The Crown Prince was still sitting in his chair, burning tobacco, waiting for them.

"It seems you had some success in there. I don't particularly want to know the details."

While Vivian might not have been curious, Evan was. Normally, the Crown Prince wasn't the type to care about what could be considered trivial matters. To others, the horrific deaths of two nobles within the Academy was significant, but to the Crown Prince, they were merely useless nobles with no abilities.

So he asked, "Why are you so concerned about Erica?"

"It's a kind of mourning. You know that well, don't you?"

"...Yes."

With those words, Evan realized why the Crown Prince was wasting energy on Erica, who was pitiful but ultimately useless. He hadn't expected her to collapse so pathetically and quickly, but it was the Imperial family who had reduced Erica's already crumbling family to dust. Perhaps even someone as seemingly inhuman as him felt a twinge of guilt.

Vivian was simply bewildered. She couldn't understand anything from their conversation.

They spoke vaguely so that those unfamiliar with the situation couldn't understand. That's why Vivian didn't particularly like nobles. But at least Erica hadn't been like that.

Vivian became melancholy once again.
